description: This plot illustrates the relationship between ultimate tensile strength (x-axis, unit: MPa, range 0-1200) and total elongation (y-axis, unit: %, range 0-100) for multiple low-density lightweight ferrous alloy systems, with corresponding density reduction levels noted: Fe-Al ferritic steels achieve 7.5-12.7% density reduction, Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy series achieve 10-20% density reduction, and Fe-Al intermetallics achieve >25% density reduction. Five material performance regions are distinguished: red region for Fe-Al ferritic steels (UTS 500-650 MPa, elongation 15-28%), blue regions for four Fe-Mn-Al-C steel types: duplex steels (α+γ, UTS 650-800 MPa, elongation 35-48%), precipitation hardening (PH) steels (α+κ, UTS 780-920 MPa, elongation 8-20%), triplex steels (γ+α+κ, UTS 800-1100 MPa, elongation 42-75%), plus purple region for Fe-Al intermetallics (UTS 950-1050 MPa, elongation 0-10%). The figure demonstrates that austenite-containing Fe-Mn-Al-C duplex and triplex steels exhibit superior strength-ductility balance and higher density reduction than ferritic steels, supporting their promising application potential for automotive lightweighting.
